title: parseChatCompletion: JSON.parse as structural type defeats API contract at trust boundary
evidence: Line 538: `data = JSON.parse(text) as typeof data;` where `data` is declared as `{ choices?: Array<{ message?: { content?: string } }>; usage?: ... }`. The `as` cast is unsound — if the API returns an unexpected shape (e.g. an array, a non-object, or `{error: ...}`) the cast passes the type checker silently. Line 546 `data.choices?.[0]?.message?.content ?? ""` then returns an empty string with no diagnostic, so a misconfigured endpoint returns silent empty responses that consume retries without signalling the real cause. This is distinct from the farm.ts JSON.parse-as-Plan finding (which the architecture lens owns) — this is the API response parse at the Zen/third-party trust boundary.
impact: A misconfigured or wrong-format API endpoint (the exact failure mode from issue #90 motivating parseChatCompletion's creation) returns `ok: true` with `content: ""` instead of an actionable error when the outer shape is not a chat-completions object. Silent empty responses exhaust retries without the operator knowing the endpoint shape is wrong.
recommendation: After parsing, add a runtime shape check: `if (!data || typeof data !== 'object' || !Array.isArray(data.choices)) { return { ok: false, error: `endpoint ${apiBaseUrl} returned unexpected shape (no 'choices' array) — ...` }; }`. This preserves the existing actionable-error goal of parseChatCompletion without touching the caller.
acceptance_criteria: A response body `{"error":"bad model"}` (no `choices`) returns `ok: false` with an actionable message naming the endpoint
A well-formed chat completion response still returns `ok: true` with the extracted content

title: mutationCheck pluggable-hook output: JSON.parse assertion on arbitrary matched substring with no object guard
evidence: Lines 903-909: a regex scans the pluggable mutation command's stdout for the last `{..."score"...}` match, then `JSON.parse(j[0]) as { score?: number; ... }`. The `as` cast performs no runtime check that the parsed value is an object — a mutation framework that emits `"score"` inside a JSON string (e.g. `"the score is 1"`) or a numeric literal would parse as a non-object and `parsed.score` would be `undefined`, silently returning `null` from mutationCheck (no mutation result). The outer `try/catch` prevents a crash but the caller gets no signal that the pluggable hook's output was malformed.
impact: A misconfigured FARM_MUTATION_CMD that emits score in an unexpected format (e.g. a JSON string rather than an object) silently disables mutation scoring for that run — the mutation guard is bypassed with no diagnostic. The failure mode is observable only in the report (mutationScore shows null for every task).
recommendation: After JSON.parse, add `if (parsed === null || typeof parsed !== 'object' || Array.isArray(parsed)) { return null; }` before reading `parsed.score`. Optionally log to stderr so a misconfigured FARM_MUTATION_CMD surfaces an actionable diagnostic.
acceptance_criteria: A mutation command that emits `"score"` as a JSON string returns null from mutationCheck
A mutation command that emits `{"score": 0.8}` still works correctly

title: mutationCheck: non-null assertion on Map.get() for originals — undocumented invariant
evidence: Line 937: `await writeFile(path.resolve(wt, c.file), originals.get(c.file)!);` — the `!` asserts `originals.get(c.file)` is non-null. The invariant (candidates are generated from `impl` which is also the key set written into `originals`) is correct but is not expressed in types. If generateMutants is ever changed to emit a candidate for a file not in `originals` (e.g. the test path, a new file the impl creates), the `!` silently passes `undefined` to `writeFile`, corrupting the worktree file with the literal string `"undefined"`.
impact: A future change to generateMutants that introduces a candidate for a file not in originals would corrupt the worktree silently. The typed alternative (explicit Map.get with a guard) would catch the regression immediately.
recommendation: Replace with `const orig = originals.get(c.file); if (orig !== undefined) await writeFile(path.resolve(wt, c.file), orig);` — this also preserves the file on a Map miss rather than overwriting it with `undefined`.
acceptance_criteria: A future generateMutants that emits an extra file does not corrupt the worktree

title: _taskboardlib.py:set_state — undocumented `state` value contract; KeyError on invalid input
evidence: Line 427: `_MARK_BY_STATE[state]` — `_MARK_BY_STATE` maps `{" ": "queued", "~": "in_progress", "x": "done", "X": "done"}` (line 89), but `set_state` takes `state` as the VALUE ("queued"/"in_progress"/"done"), not the key. The inverse dict is `_MARK_BY_STATE = {" ": "queued", ...}` while `set_state` uses `_MARK_BY_STATE` as `{"queued": " ", ...}` — wait, let me re-examine: line 337 `_MARK_BY_STATE = {"queued": " ", "in_progress": "~", "done": "x"}` (the writer uses this for state->marker). Line 427 `f"- [{_MARK_BY_STATE[state]}] {rest}"` — if `state` is any string other than "queued", "in_progress", "done", this raises `KeyError`. The coding standards mandate "Never raise on malformed user input". The function docstring does not list the valid values.
impact: A caller passing an unexpected state string (e.g. "pending", a typo, or a localized value) raises a KeyError that propagates out of set_state. Since set_state is called from the /ca:task command path (a user-facing interactive flow), this crashes the hook session rather than degrading gracefully.
recommendation: Add a guard: `if state not in _MARK_BY_STATE: return text` (degrade silently) or raise with a clear message listing valid values. Document the valid set in the function's docstring. The header API line should read `set_state(text, target, state, today, *, assign=None) -> str  # state in {"queued","in_progress","done"}`.
acceptance_criteria: set_state with an unknown state string does not raise KeyError
The docstring names the valid state values
The file header API line documents the constraint

title: _taskboardlib.py:promote — silent fallthrough for unknown `mode`; undocumented valid values
evidence: Lines 525-526: `if mode == "interactive": return PromoteResult(fresh, board, questions, [], False)`. Any mode that is not "interactive" silently executes the auto branch (applies all candidates and returns). A caller passing `mode="dry-run"` or a misspelled `mode="autommatic"` gets auto-apply behavior with no diagnostic. The docstring says 'mode="interactive" returns ... mode="auto" applies' but does not state that unknown modes are equivalent to auto.
impact: A typo in the `mode` argument silently applies all candidates to the board and question file instead of returning them for user confirmation. This is a mutation of persistent state (open-tasks.md / open-questions.md) triggered by a silent type confusion.
recommendation: Add an explicit guard: `if mode not in ("interactive", "auto"): raise ValueError(f"promote: unknown mode {mode!r}; expected 'interactive' or 'auto'")`. Given the 'never raise on malformed user input' rule applies to hook-stdin paths (not library logic), raising here is appropriate since promote is a library function. Alternatively degrade to auto with a `warn()` call. Document valid modes in the docstring.
acceptance_criteria: promote with mode='dry-run' raises ValueError or emits a warn() rather than silently applying
The docstring explicitly states that only 'interactive' and 'auto' are valid

title: run.ts:NetPolicy branded open-string type — typos silently accepted, offline guarantee not enforced by type
evidence: Line 58: `export type NetPolicy = "offline" | (string & {});` — the branded open-string type is intentional (allows future policies) but means TypeScript accepts any string literal where NetPolicy is expected without a warning. A caller passing `"Offline"` (capital O) or `"ofline"` (typo) compiles cleanly; the offline guarantee (`--network none`) then silently never fires and the container runs with the default bridge network. The comment says 'An unknown policy is passed through without forcing isolation.'
impact: A typo in a caller's NetPolicy argument silently grants the container network access instead of airgapping it. The offline guarantee — which is described as GUARANTEED — is type-level invisible to the caller. No compile-time or runtime feedback.
recommendation: For the known-safe policies that runContainer/buildRunArgs handle today, narrow to a closed union: `export type NetPolicy = "offline" | "clone-then-cut" | "egress-allowlist"`. The open-string escape hatch can be documented separately or gated behind an `| (string & {})` only at the T-10 layer that explicitly handles pass-through. Add a `default` branch in buildRunArgs that warns (stderr) when an unrecognized policy is passed.
acceptance_criteria: Passing NetPolicy = 'Offline' produces a TypeScript compile error
The offline guarantee is enforced by type narrowing, not just documentation

title: _hooklib.py: public API undocumented in header (coding-standards violation)
evidence: The coding standards (`coding-standards.md`, Python section) require: 'Document the public API in the file header as `name(args) -> type` lines.' `_hooklib.py`'s header block (lines 1-35) has NO API listing — it describes the module's purpose and design but omits the `name(args) -> type` listing entirely. Contrast with `_taskboardlib.py` (lines 29-38) and `_metricslib.py` (lines 14-34) which both comply. _hooklib.py exports at least 15 public functions (utf8_stdio, norm_path, frontmatter_enabled, arbiter_active, read_input, tool_input, project_root, repo_rel, line_digest, content_digest, migration_globs, is_migration_path, is_ci_path, is_deploy_path, marker_fresh, scope_globs, path_in_globs, block, remind, warn). Similarly _sloplib.py has no header API listing.
impact: A caller adding a new hook or modifying an existing one cannot see the contract (argument types, return types) without reading every function body. This is the surface through which untrusted hook payloads flow — a misuse of `tool_input(data)` or `repo_rel(fpath, root)` by a hook author is invisible at a glance.
recommendation: Add the `name(args) -> type` API block to _hooklib.py and _sloplib.py headers, following the _taskboardlib.py/metricslib.py pattern. Minimum: the public functions used by multiple hooks (read_input, tool_input, arbiter_active, block, remind, warn, repo_rel, is_migration_path, is_ci_path, is_deploy_path, marker_fresh).
acceptance_criteria: _hooklib.py header lists all public functions as `name(args) -> type` lines
_sloplib.py header lists its public functions similarly
